headline: Baleh Dam Threatens 3,000 Iban Ancestral Burial Sites
context: The Baleh Hydroelectric Dam in Sarawak, expected to flood 6,541 hectares by 2028, will submerge an estimated 3,000 Iban ancestral burial sites and 15 longhouses.

cards.big: Sarawak Energy calls Baleh Dam a 'clean energy milestone' for 1,285MW capacity.
cards.sub: For 15 longhouse communities, it means the permanent submersion of 3,000 ancestral burial sites.

cards.big: Compensation offers average RM15,000 per family.
cards.sub: No valuation framework exists for ancestral burial grounds under Sarawak's Land Code.

cards.big: Baleh repeats the Bakun model — same developer, same affected communities.
cards.sub: Lessons from Sungai Asap's failed resettlement have not been formally incorporated into Baleh planning.

cards.big: Sarawak's electricity demand grows at 4% annually.
cards.sub: Current installed capacity already exceeds demand by 45%. Baleh is for export, not domestic need.

cards.big: If the power is for export, why are local communities bearing the cost?
Hydroelectric development is legitimate. But when surplus power is exported while affected communities lose irreplaceable heritage, the distribution of costs and benefits is unjust.
